era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its final decade. Athens, despite economic strain and military losses, maintains naval operations and seeks to sustain the conflict. Sparta, with Persian financial support, builds its naval power. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion under Hannibal continues to advance. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains stable. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts and the consolidation of state power.

events.title: Alcibiades returns to Athens
events.description: Alcibiades, the controversial Athenian general and politician, returned to Athens after years of exile and service with Sparta and Persia. He had been exiled in 415 BCE following the Sicilian Expedition scandal. His return was politically significant, as he represented a potential military solution to Athens' problems. However, his presence was divisive, and his influence would be short-lived. He was given command of the Athenian fleet and achieved some naval successes, but his unpopularity and the political instability in Athens limited his effectiveness.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Alcibiades' return and his command of the fleet. The dating to 407 BCE is secure in the historical tradition.

events.title: Lysander appointed Spartan admiral
events.description: Lysander was appointed as the Spartan admiral (nauarch) and given command of the Spartan fleet. This appointment proved to be a turning point in the Peloponnesian War. Lysander was an exceptionally capable commander who would prove instrumental in Sparta's eventual victory. He used Persian financial support effectively to build and maintain a superior fleet, and he employed diplomatic and psychological tactics to undermine Athenian morale and alliances. His appointment marked the beginning of Sparta's naval ascendancy.
key_figures: Cyrus the Younger (Persian satrap, supporter of Lysander)
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Lysander's appointment. The dating to 407 BCE is secure, though some sources place it in 406 BCE. The consensus favors 407 BCE.

events.title: Battle of Notium
events.description: Alcibiades' fleet was defeated by Lysander's Spartan fleet near Notium in Ionia. This was a significant setback for Athens and a demonstration of Lysander's superior naval tactics and leadership. The defeat undermined Alcibiades' political position in Athens and contributed to his eventual exile. The battle showed that Spartan naval power, backed by Persian gold, was now competitive with Athens' traditional naval superiority.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides a detailed account of the battle. The dating to 407 BCE is secure.

events.title: Alcibiades exiled again from Athens
events.description: Following the defeat at Notium, Alcibiades was again exiled from Athens. The Athenian assembly, frustrated by his military setback and his controversial political history, voted to remove him from command and exile him. Alcibiades fled to Thrace and later to Phrygia, where he lived in exile until his death. His second exile marked the end of his political influence in Athens and removed one of the few figures who might have provided effective military leadership in the final years of the war.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Alcibiades' exile. The dating to 407 BCE is secure.

events.title: Carthaginian campaign in Sicily continues
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces continued their military campaigns in Sicily, capturing additional Greek cities and consolidating Carthaginian control over the western and central parts of the island. The campaigns were marked by brutal sieges and the destruction of Greek cities. The ongoing Carthaginian threat destabilized Greek Sicily and contributed to the political fragmentation that would eventually lead to Dionysius I's rise to power.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides accounts of Carthaginian campaigns in Sicily during this period, but specific events for 407 BCE are not always clearly dated. The general context of ongoing Carthaginian expansion is secure.

events.title: Cyrus the Younger appointed satrap of Lydia
events.description: Cyrus the Younger, son of Artaxerxes II, was appointed satrap of Lydia and other territories in western Asia Minor. This appointment gave him significant power and resources, which he would later use to support Lysander and the Spartan fleet against Athens. Cyrus' position as satrap made him a key player in Persian-Greek relations during the final years of the Peloponnesian War. His later rebellion (401 BCE) would have major consequences for the Persian Empire and Greece.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon mentions Cyrus' position as satrap, but the exact date of his appointment is not clearly specified. The traditional dating places it around 407 BCE, but some sources suggest 408 or 406 BCE. The year 407 BCE is conventional but not definitively confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Alcibiades' return to Athens is sometimes placed in 408 BCE in alternative chronologies. The Battle of Notium is occasionally dated to 406 BCE. Lysander's appointment as admiral is sometimes dated to 406 BCE rather than 407 BCE. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history, particularly when sources are not always consistent. The exact date of Cyrus the Younger's appointment as satrap is uncertain and may have occurred in 408 or 406 BCE rather than 407 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 407 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary account of the Peloponnesian War. Plutarch's biographies of Alcibiades and Lysander provide additional details but were written centuries later. Sicilian history relies on Diodorus Siculus, who wrote much later. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year, relying on Greek accounts and limited Persian sources. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the record is particularly focused on military and political events in the Aegean region.

graph_edges.note: Alcibiades' return to Athens led to his command of the fleet, which resulted in the defeat at Notium
The defeat at Notium led to Alcibiades' second exile from Athens
Lysander's appointment as admiral set the stage for his decisive victory at Aegospotami
Cyrus the Younger's position as satrap enabled him to support Lysander with Persian resources
